title: Add mobile quality scaling
overview: Integrate the existing quality tier system into the live render pipeline so that mobile and low-capability devices receive reduced scene complexity (fewer particles, lower resolution scale, disabled sparkle) while keeping the visual output recognizably similar across tiers. The quality.ts module and its tests already exist; the work is wiring it through scene init, the render loop, resize handling, and the geometry systems.

steps.title: Wire computeQuality into main.ts
steps.description: Uncomment the computeQuality import in main.ts. After readSignals() resolves in the geoPromise.then callback, call computeQuality(signals) to obtain the QualityProfile. Store the profile and pass it to downstream consumers (scene, geometry systems, render loop).
details: Uncomment `import { computeQuality } from './visual/quality'` (line 17)
After `const signals = readSignals()` (line 43), add `const quality = computeQuality(signals)`
Log quality tier: `console.debug('[EAVI] quality tier:', quality.tier)`
Pass quality profile to deps: `deps.quality = quality`

steps.title: Apply resolutionScale to the renderer
steps.description: Use the QualityProfile.resolutionScale to control effective render resolution. Modify initScene to accept resolutionScale and apply it to the pixel ratio. Update attachResizeHandler to preserve the scaled pixel ratio on resize.
details: Change the unused `_resolutionScale` parameter to active: use it to scale the pixel ratio — `renderer.setPixelRatio(Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio, 2) * resolutionScale)`
In `attachResizeHandler` (resize.ts), accept and store `resolutionScale` so resize events re-apply the same scaled pixel ratio

In main.ts, call `initScene(app)` immediately (before quality is known) with default scale 1.0, then after quality resolves, call `renderer.setPixelRatio(Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio, 2) * quality.resolutionScale)` and `renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight)` to re-apply

steps.title: Conditionally disable antialias on low tier
steps.description: Antialias is expensive on mobile GPUs. Since the renderer is created before quality is known, and antialias cannot be changed after creation, consider the DPR as a quick pre-quality heuristic: disable antialias when DPR is 1 and touch is detected. Alternatively, accept that antialias is set at creation and defer this optimization.
details: Add a `disableAntialias` boolean parameter to initScene (default false)
In main.ts, use a quick pre-check: `const quickTouch = 'ontouchstart' in window || navigator.maxTouchPoints > 0` and `const lowDPR = window.devicePixelRatio <= 1`
Pass `disableAntialias: quickTouch && lowDPR` to initScene
In initScene, set `antialias: !disableAntialias`

steps.title: Add QualityProfile to LoopDeps and render loop
steps.description: Extend LoopDeps to include an optional QualityProfile. The render loop will use it to pass quality-aware configuration to geometry systems on init.
details: Import QualityProfile type from '../visual/quality'
Add `quality?: QualityProfile | null` to LoopDeps interface

When initializing geometry system (line 147-149), the quality profile is already baked into the geometry system config via main.ts, so no render loop changes are strictly needed beyond carrying the reference

steps.title: Uncomment and wire geometry systems with quality-driven config
steps.description: Uncomment the geometry system imports in main.ts. Pass QualityProfile.maxParticles and enableSparkle into the particle field and wave field factories. Wire the mode manager into the render loop deps.
details: Uncomment imports for createParticleField, createWaveField, createModeManager (lines 14-16)
After quality is computed, create geometry systems with quality config:
`const particles = createParticleField({ maxParticles: quality.maxParticles, enableSparkle: quality.enableSparkle })`
`const waves = createWaveField({ maxWaves: quality.tier === 'low' ? 10 : 20, enableShimmer: quality.enableSparkle })`
`const modeManager = createModeManager([{ name: 'particles', factory: () => particles }, { name: 'waves', factory: () => waves }])`
Set `deps.geometrySystem = modeManager`

steps.title: Scale wave count by quality tier
steps.description: The WaveField already accepts maxWaves config. Ensure the low tier gets fewer waves (e.g., 10 instead of 20) to reduce draw calls and vertex processing on mobile.
details: No code changes needed in waveField.ts itself — the maxWaves config is already respected
The scaling is applied at construction time in main.ts (step 5) by passing a reduced maxWaves for low tier

steps.title: Update resize handler to respect resolution scale
steps.description: Modify attachResizeHandler to accept and preserve the resolution scale factor so that window resizes don't reset the pixel ratio to an unscaled value.
details: Add optional `resolutionScale?: number` parameter to attachResizeHandler
In the onResize callback, apply `renderer.setPixelRatio(Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio, 2) * (resolutionScale ?? 1.0))`

In main.ts, after quality resolves, detach the old handler and re-attach with the quality-aware scale: store the detach function and call it, then `attachResizeHandler(renderer, camera, quality.resolutionScale)`

steps.title: Add integration tests for quality-driven rendering
steps.description: Add tests verifying that the quality tier flows through to geometry system configuration and that resolution scale is applied to the renderer.
details: Existing tests (T-025-01 through T-025-08) already cover tier computation — these pass and should not be modified
Add new integration-level tests:
T-025-09: Verify createParticleField respects maxParticles from low-tier quality profile (init with low density, check particle count <= 150)
T-025-10: Verify createWaveField respects reduced maxWaves (pass maxWaves: 10, check wave count <= 10)
T-025-11: Verify resolution scale affects renderer pixel ratio (mock renderer, call setPixelRatio with scaled value, assert)

steps.title: Verify build, no console errors, and acceptance criteria
steps.description: Run the production build, verify no TypeScript errors, run all tests, and manually verify the acceptance criteria.
details: Run `npm run build` — must pass with no errors
Run `npx vitest run` — all tests must pass
Verify: lower-capability device signals produce reduced scene complexity (particle count, resolution scale)
Verify: touch input remains supported (pointer.ts handles both mouse and touch, unchanged)
Verify: no new console errors on load
Verify: visual output stays recognizably similar (same palette, cadence, structure — only density and resolution change)

acceptance_criteria_mapping.Lower-capability devices use reduced sce: Steps 1, 2, 3, 5, 6 — quality tier drives maxParticles (150/350/600), resolutionScale (0.5/0.75/1.0), maxWaves (10/20), enableSparkle, and antialias
acceptance_criteria_mapping.Touch input remains supported: No changes to src/input/pointer.ts — touch events are already handled. curveSoftness mapping already adapts for touch. Step 3 uses touch detection only for antialias heuristic.
acceptance_criteria_mapping.Mobile browsers do not crash on load: Steps 2, 3 reduce GPU load (lower pixel ratio, disabled antialias). Step 5 reduces particle/wave counts. Scene renders a simple placeholder immediately before async quality computation.
acceptance_criteria_mapping.Visual output stays recognizably similar: Same palette (geo-derived), same cadence (time-derived), same evolution curves. Only density, particle count, resolution, and sparkle differ — the scene identity is preserved across tiers.

risks.risk: Renderer is created before quality is known (sync init vs async geo+signals)
risks.mitigation: Create renderer with default settings immediately for fast first paint, then re-apply resolutionScale and pixel ratio after quality resolves. Antialias uses a quick sync heuristic (DPR + touch check) that doesn't require full quality computation.

risks.risk: Geometry systems have TODO draw methods (Canvas 2D not yet ported to Three.js)
risks.mitigation: This story wires quality into the system configs and init. The draw() implementations are no-ops but will correctly receive quality-scaled counts. The placeholder mesh continues to render. Draw implementation is a separate story.
